title: Assessment Items - External Disaster Response (Code Yellow)

items.question: What must be verified first when the facility receives a report from an external party about a disaster?
choices: Only the name of the reporting person.
The source of the report, type and magnitude of the disaster, disaster location, and approximate number and type of expected casualties.
Only the nearest available ambulance unit.
Only whether media coverage has started.
items.rationale: The first step is to verify the report source and the key operational details of the disaster.

items.question: How should Code Yellow be activated inside the facility?
choices: By sending a private email to administration only.
Through the internal overhead speaker system and by signaling emergency security teams through wireless communication devices.
By waiting until ambulances arrive at the gate.
Through a handwritten note at the emergency entrance.
items.rationale: The procedure requires overhead announcement and signaling to the assigned response teams.

items.question: What should happen immediately after Code Yellow activation?
choices: The facility external disaster emergency plan should be activated.
All departments should close independently without coordination.
Only the front gate should be secured.
Patients should be discharged immediately.
items.rationale: Code Yellow requires activation of the external disaster emergency plan and relevant preparedness procedures.

items.question: Which entities should security coordinate with to increase readiness in the receiving area?
choices: Only visitor services and housekeeping.
Ambulance command, emergency departments, and the planning and emergency preparedness unit.
Only the mortuary unit.
Only the outpatient clinic supervisor.
items.rationale: These are the key operational entities involved in preparing the receiving area during an external disaster.

items.question: What is required regarding ambulance access points during the disaster response?
choices: Keep all traffic routes open in both directions without control.
Prepare ambulance access points, assign one-way movement paths, and facilitate arrival to the triage area.
Close all access points until triage is completed.
Use only visitor parking entrances for ambulance traffic.
items.rationale: Controlled one-way ambulance access is part of the required movement management.

items.question: How should minor or low-severity cases be handled at the main external gate?
choices: Direct them to other nearby hospitals under medical supervision according to the external emergency plan.
Admit all minor cases to the main emergency area immediately.
Turn them away without documentation.
Send them to the facility cafeteria until staff are available.
items.rationale: Minor cases may be redirected to nearby hospitals according to the external emergency plan and medical supervision.

items.question: What access-control rule applies to ambulance and emergency departments during Code Yellow?
choices: Access should be allowed to all companions for reassurance.
Only authorized persons are permitted to enter ambulance and emergency departments.
Any staff member may enter if they know a patient.
Only visitors with badges are restricted.
items.rationale: The policy requires strict access control and authorized-entry-only rules.

items.question: Which areas must remain fully covered by CCTV during the event?
choices: Only staff break rooms and pharmacy storage.
Ambulance and emergency zones, including triage and patient transport areas.
Only the administration lobby.
Only the main exit gate.
items.rationale: The procedure requires full monitoring coverage of ambulance, emergency, triage, and transport areas.

items.question: What must security prevent around the triage and ambulance/emergency perimeter?
choices: Only staff discussion.
Crowd gathering and photography.
Ambulance communication.
Patient transport documentation.
items.rationale: Crowd gathering and photography around sensitive response areas must be prevented.

items.question: Where should families of casualties and injured persons be directed?
choices: To the designated authority in the facility for information and coordination.
Directly into the triage area.
To the ambulance parking zone.
To any available clinical room.
items.rationale: Families should be directed to the designated authority for controlled information and coordination.

items.question: What is required when handling deceased persons during the disaster response?
choices: Follow strict procedures for receiving, transferring, and handling deceased persons with authorized specialists and the mortuary.
Transfer all deceased persons through public entrances.
Store bodies temporarily in the triage area.
Handle the process only after all living casualties are discharged.
items.rationale: The policy requires strict controlled handling of deceased persons with authorized specialists and mortuary coordination.

items.question: What must happen to records and belongings related to casualties, injured persons, and deceased persons?
choices: They should be preserved according to facility policies and procedures.
They may be released to companions immediately without documentation.
They should be discarded after patient transfer.
They are only preserved if police request them later.
items.rationale: All related records and belongings must be preserved according to policy.

items.question: What is required if the activated disaster scenario involves biological, chemical, or radiological weapons?
choices: Security staff may respond normally without special equipment.
Security personnel must implement required PPE measures and follow Hazmat Response Team instructions.
Only medical staff need protection in such scenarios.
The triage area should remain unchanged without protective controls.
items.rationale: These scenarios require PPE and compliance with Hazmat team instructions.

items.question: What should security do when the activated external disaster scenario involves epidemic or infectious disease events?
choices: Ignore PPE and rely only on distancing.
Implement PPE, follow precautionary procedures, and coordinate with Infection Control according to public health guidance.
Leave all infectious disease control to outpatient clinics only.
Suspend all screening and isolation processes.
items.rationale: Epidemic scenarios require PPE, precautionary procedures, and coordination with Infection Control.

items.question: What support-related responsibilities apply regarding PPE and staff protection readiness?
choices: Communicate with the relevant authority to provide PPE and ensure security personnel have the required vaccinations when applicable.
Wait for staff to arrange their own protection individually.
Request PPE only after casualties begin arriving.
Vaccination status is irrelevant to disaster response planning.
items.rationale: Security leadership must coordinate PPE supply and verify required vaccination readiness where applicable.

items.question: When may Code Red be announced during activation of the external disaster plan?
choices: Only if the disaster is fully under control.
When casualties exceed the facility response capacity or when an internal disaster such as a fire occurs.
Only if visitors demand additional security presence.
Only after the final report is completed.
items.rationale: Code Red may be declared during the external disaster response if capacity is exceeded or if an internal disaster occurs.

items.question: What should security do after the end of the internal or external disaster?
choices: Leave the area immediately because the event is over.
Continue security presence, secure the area, facilitate ambulance movement, and support return to normal operations only after approved security directions.
Reopen all routes without coordination.
Stop all post-event support and documentation.
items.rationale: Post-incident security presence remains necessary until approved normalization steps are completed.

items.question: What is the required post-incident improvement process after the disaster ends?
choices: Archive the event with no operational review.
Evaluate security staff and the security plan, identify weaknesses and gaps, extract lessons learned, circulate them, and submit recommendations to facility leadership.
Destroy temporary records after the last ambulance leaves.
Limit review only to the triage team.
items.rationale: A full lessons-learned and improvement review is required after the event.
